Lasagna

Description

Lasagna is a wide, flat sheet of pasta. Lasagna can refer to either the type of noodle or to the typical lasagna dish which is a dish made with several layers of lasagna sheets with sauce and other ingredients, such as meats and cheese, in between the lasagna noodles.

Ingredients

  • Meat
  • Onion and garlic
  • Tomato products
  • Sugar
  • Spices and seasonings
  • Lasagna noodles
  • Cheeses
  • Egg

Steps

  1. Make the meat sauce.
  2. Cook the noodles.
  3. Make the ricotta mixture.
  4. Layer the lasagna according to the recipe instructions.
  5. Cover with foil and bake.
  6. Let the lasagna rest before serving.

...’s theresurrection of the dead. The resurrection of the dead is a belief that at the end of time, God will raise the dead and judge them. This is a central tenet in Christianity and Islam. In Christianity, it is tied to the resurrection of Jesus Christ. In Islam, it is part of the day of judgment. This belief gives hope that death is not the end, and that there is life after death. It also implies accountability for one's actions in this life. The resurrection of the body suggests that our physical bodies are important to our identity and that we will be raised in a perfected form. This doctrine has been a source of comfort for believers facing persecution or martyrdom, knowing that their death is not the end. It also motivates ethical living, as believers know they will give account for their actions. The resurrection of the dead is a profound mystery that points to the power of God over life and death. It is a doctrine that has shaped art, literature, and the practices of burial and mourning in cultures influenced by these religions. The resurrection hope has been a powerful motivator for missionary work and martyrdom. It stands as a counter to philosophies that see death as the final end or that advocate for the immortality of the soul without the body. The resurrection affirms the goodness of the material world and the body. It promises that injustice will be addressed and that death will be defeated. This doctrine has been debated and refined through centuries of theological reflection, but it remains a core hope for billions. The resurrection of the dead is not just a future event but shapes how believers live today, in hope and responsibility. It is a doctrine that speaks to the value of each human life and the promise of redemption. The resurrection of the dead is thus a cornerstone of faith for many, offering hope beyond the grave.# The Resurrection of Jesus Christ

The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the cornerstone of Christian faith. According to Christian belief, Jesus was crucified, died, and was buried, but on the third day, he rose from the dead. This event is celebrated on Easter Sunday. The resurrection is seen as proof of Jesus' divinity and the promise of eternal life for believers. The Gospels provide accounts of the empty tomb and Jesus' appearances to his followers after his resurrection. This event transformed the disciples from fearful followers into bold proclaimers of the Gospel. The resurrection is central to Christian theology, affirming that Jesus conquered death, offering salvation and hope. It validates Jesus' teachings and sacrifice, serving as the foundation for the Christian belief in life after death. Without the resurrection, Christianity would lose its core message of redemption and eternal life. Thus, the resurrection is not just a historical claim but a profound spiritual reality for Christians worldwide.# 2nd Grade Math Worksheets Multiplication

# 1. Introduction

The problem of reconstructing a permutation from partial information is a fundamental problem in combinatorics and computer science. In this paper, we consider the problem of reconstructing a permutation $\pi$ of $[n] = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ given the set of its $k$-subpermutations, i.e., the set of all permutations that can be obtained by restricting $\pi$ to a $k$-element subset of $[n]$. 

More formally, for a permutation $\pi$ of $[n]$, the $k$-deck of $\pi$, denoted $D_k(\pi)$, is the multiset of all $k$-subpermutations of $\pi$. Two permutations $\pi$ and $\sigma$ are said to be $k$-equivalent if $D_k(\pi) = D_k(\sigma)$. The reconstruction number $rn_k(n)$ is the minimum number such that any two distinct permutations of $[n]$ can be distinguished by some $k$-deck, i.e., they are not $k$-equivalent. 

The problem was first introduced by Levenshtein [1] who proved that $rn_3(n) = n$ for all $n \geq 3$. That is, any two distinct permutations of $[n]$ can be distinguished by their $3$-decks. He also conjectured that $rn_k(n) = n$ for all $k \geq 3$ and $n$ sufficiently large. This conjecture was disproved by Krasikov and Roditty [2] who showed that for $k=4$, there exist distinct permutations of $[n]$ that are $4$-equivalent for infinitely many $n$. 

In this paper, we study the problem of reconstructing permutations from their $k$-decks with additional information. Specifically, we consider the case where we are given the multiset of pairs $(i, \pi(i))$ for each position $i$, i.e., the values of the permutation at each position. We call this additional information the position-value pairs. 

Our main result is that with the position-value pairs, we can reconstruct the permutation from its $k$-deck for $k=2$. That is, given the $2$-deck and the position-value pairs, we can uniquely determine the permutation. 

We also consider the case where we are given the multiset of values $\pi(i)$ for each position $i$, but not the correspondence between positions and values. We call this the value multiset. We show that with the value multiset, we can reconstruct the permutation from its $k$-deck for $k=3$. 

These results demonstrate that even a small amount of additional information can significantly reduce the reconstruction number. 

# 2. Preliminaries

Let $S_n$ denote the set of all permutations of $[n]$. For a permutation $\pi \in S_n$, and a subset $S \subseteq [n]$, let $\pi|_S$ denote the permutation obtained by restricting $\pi$ to the elements of $S$. The $k$-deck of $\pi$, denoted $D_k(\pi)$, is the multiset $\{ \pi|_S : S \subseteq [n], |S| = k \}$.

We are given additional information about $\pi$:

1. The position-value pairs: a multiset $\{(i, \pi(i)) : i \in [n]\}$. Note that this is equivalent to knowing the permutation $\pi$ itself, but we are considering the scenario where we are given the deck and this multiset separately, and we need to reconstruct $\pi$ from both.

2. The value multiset: a multiset $\{ \pi(i) : i \in [n] \}$. This is equivalent to knowing the set of values in the permutation, but since it's a multiset, we know the frequency of each value, but not which position it appears in.

Our goal is to reconstruct $\pi$ from $D_k(\pi)$ and the additional information.

If you have context or a specific question about this text, clarify for further analysis!# 使用 Python 中的 Plotly 绘制小提琴图

> 原文:[https://www . geeksforgeeks . org/绘图-小提琴-绘图-使用-绘图-in-python/](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/plotting-violin-plot-using-plotly-in-python/)

**Plotly** 是一个 Python 库，用于设计图形，尤其是交互式图形。它可以绘制各种图形和图表，如直方图、条形图、箱线图、展开图等。它主要用于数据分析和财务分析。plotly 是一个交互式可视化库。

## 小提琴情节

小提琴图类似于箱线图，但提供了数据分布。它显示了数据的概率密度。小提琴图用于可视化数据的分布及其概率密度。小提琴图比箱线图更清晰、更深入地分析数据。

## 创建小提琴情节

小提琴图可以使用 plotly.express 类创建。plotly.express 模块是 plotly.py 的简化接口，它提供了各种类型绘图的简单语法。

> **语法:**plot . express . violin(arguments)
> 
> **参数:**
> 
> *   **数据帧:**数据帧的名称
> *   **x:** 数据帧中的列名
> *   **y:** 数据帧中的列名
> *   **颜色:**数据帧中的列名
> *   **标题:**绘图的标题字符串
> *   **宽度:**

Here's a concise summary of the books with key details:

### Mystery: The Silent Forest (2021)
**Author:** Elena Vance  
**Setting:** Small town bordering an ancient forest  
**Characters:**  
- Detective Harris (Protagonist)  
- Mayor Thompson (Suspect)  
- Old Man Jenkins (Mysterious Figure)  
**Plot:** Detective Harris investigates disappearances linked to an ancient forest.

### Science Fiction: Stars Beyond Reach (2023)
**Author:** Marcus Ridley  
**Setting:** Desert planet LV-426  
**Characters:**  
- Captain Ava Solace (Leader)  
- Dr. Finn Carter (Scientist)  
- Engineer Jax Moro (Tech Specialist)  
**Plot:** Stranded crew discovers alien tech that could save humanity.

### Historical Fiction: Whispers of the Past (2020)
**Author:** Clara Bennett  
**Setting:** Victorian-era manor house  
**Characters:**  
- Eleanor Grey (Protagonist)  
- Lord Edmund Grey (Antagonist)  
- Mrs. Potts (Housekeeper)  
**Plot:** Young woman uncovers family secrets in her ancestral home.

### Key Observations:
1. **Genre Diversity:** Mystery, Sci-Fi, and Historical Fiction  
2. **Common Themes:** Hidden truths (forest secrets/alien tech/family history)  
3. **Character Archetypes:**  
   - Investigators (Detective/Eleanor)  
   - Authority figures (Mayor/Captain/Lord)  
   - Knowledge holders (Jenkins/Scientist/Housekeeper)  
4. **Settings:** All feature isolated/confined locations enhancing tension

# How to: Create Signed Friend Assemblies (C#)
This example shows how to use friend assemblies with assemblies that have strong names. Both assemblies must be strong named. Although both assemblies in this example use the same keys, you could use different keys for two assemblies.  
  
### To create a signed assembly and a friend assembly  
  
1.  Open a command prompt.  
  
2.  Use the following sequence of commands with the Strong Name tool to generate a keyfile and to display its public key. For more information, see [Sn.exe (Strong Name Tool)](../../../../framework/tools/sn-exe-strong-name-tool.md)).  
  
    1.  Generate a strong-name key for this example and store it in the file FriendAssemblies.snk:

3. **Technical artifacts:** Includes code-like snippets (`_D哦etry`, `()];`, `\mathcal`, `$ statements`).
4. **No discernible intent:** Lacks a clear question, statement, or theme.

**Conclusion:** This is not a valid query or meaningful text. If you encountered this unexpectedly, it may be data corruption. For assistance, please provide a clear question in English or your preferred language.---

## Loading and preprocessing the data

```r
library(dplyr)
library(lattice)
activity <- read.csv("activity.csv")
activity$date <- as.Date(activity$date)
```


## What is mean total number of steps taken per day?

```r
steps_per_day <- activity %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(total_steps = sum(steps, na.rm = TRUE))
hist(steps_per_day$total_steps, breaks = 10, main = "Histogram of Steps per Day", xlab = "Steps per Day")
```

![](PA1_template_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png)<!-- -->

```r
mean_steps_per_day <- mean(steps_per_day$total_steps)
median_steps_per_day <- median(steps_per_day$total_steps)
```
The mean total number of steps per day is 9354.2295082 and the median is 10395.

## What is the average daily activity pattern?

```r
steps_per_interval <- activity %>% group_by(interval) %>% summarize(mean_steps = mean(steps, na.rm = TRUE))
plot(steps_per_interval$interval, steps_per_interval$mean_steps, type = "l", xlab = "Interval", ylab = "Mean Steps", main = "Average Steps per Interval")
```

![](PA1_template_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png)<!-- -->

```r
max_interval <- steps_per_interval$interval[which.max(steps_per_interval$mean_steps)]
```
The interval with the maximum average steps is 835.

## Imputing missing values

```r
num_missing <- sum(is.na(activity$steps))
```
The number of missing values is 2304.

```r
activity_imputed <- activity
for (i in 1:nrow(activity_imputed)) {
    if (is.na(activity_imputed$steps[i])) {
        activity_imputed$steps[i] <- steps_per_interval$mean_steps[steps_per_interval$interval == activity_imputed$interval[i]]
    }
}
steps_per_day_imputed <- activity_imputed %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(total_steps = sum(steps))
hist(steps_per_day_imputed$total_steps, breaks = 10, main = "Histogram of Steps per Day (Imputed)", xlab = "Steps per Day")
```

![](PA1_template_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png)<!-- -->

```r
mean_steps_per_day_imputed <- mean(steps_per_day_imputed$total_steps)
median_steps_per_day_imputed <- median(steps_per_day_imputed$total_steps)
```
The mean total number of steps per day (imputed) is 1.0766189\times 10^{4} and the median is 1.0766189\times 10^{4}.

# Sustainable Living Guide Implementation Plan

## Problem Analysis
The user submitted a JSON document containing an article about sustainable living practices. The document includes metadata (title, author, date, category, tags), article content with numbered tips, comments section, and engagement metrics.

## Solution Plan
We'll create a Python script that:
1. Parses the JSON document
2. Extracts key information
3. Generates a formatted report containing:
   - Article metadata summary
   - Numbered sustainability tips
   - Comment highlights
   - Engagement metrics summary

## Implementation
```python
import json
from datetime import datetime

def parse_sustainability_data(json_data):
    """
    Parses JSON data about sustainable living and returns formatted information.
    """
    # Load JSON data
    data = json.loads(json_data)
    
    # Extract metadata
    title = data.get('title', 'Untitled Article')
    author = data.get('author', 'Unknown Author')
    date_str = data.get('date_published', '')
    try:
        date_obj = datetime.strptime(date_str, '%Y-%m-%d')
        formatted_date = date_obj.strftime('%B %d, %Y')
    except:
        formatted_date = "Unknown date"
    
    # Extract tips from content
    tips = []
    content = data.get('content', '')
    # Simple extraction of numbered tips (assuming format "1. ...")
    lines = content.split('\n')
    for line in lines:
        if line.strip() and line.strip()[0].isdigit() and '.' in line:
            tip = line.split('.', 1)[1].strip()
            tips.append(tip)
    
    # Process comments
    comments = []
    for comment in data.get('comments', []):
        user = comment.get('user', 'Anonymous')
        comment_text = comment.get('comment', '')
        timestamp = comment.get('timestamp', '')
        try:
            comment_time = datetime.strptime(timestamp, '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')
            formatted_time = comment_time.strftime('%b %d at %I:%M %p')
        except:
            formatted_time = "Unknown time"
        comments.append({
            'user': user,
            'comment': comment_text,
            'time': formatted_time
        })
    
    # Engagement metrics
    engagement = data.get('engagement', {})
    likes = engagement.get('likes', 0)
    shares = engagement.get('shares', 0)
    views = engagement.get('views', 0)
    
    # Formatting the report
    report = f"""

## 1 Introduction

Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing, demonstrating remarkable capabilities in tasks such as text generation, translation, and question answering Brown et al. (2020); OpenAI (2023). However, deploying LLMs in real-world applications presents significant challenges due to their computational demands and latency constraints. To address these challenges, quantization techniques have emerged as a promising solution, reducing model size and inference latency while preserving accuracy Frantar et al. (2022); Dettmers et al. (2023); Xiao et al. (2023).

Quantization involves converting model weights and activations from high-precision floating-point representations to lower-precision integers. This reduction in precision leads to decreased memory footprint and faster computation, enabling LLMs to run efficiently on resource-constrained devices. However, quantization inevitably introduces errors that can degrade model performance, particularly in complex reasoning tasks.

Recent studies have explored quantization-aware training (QAT) Hubara et al. (2017) and post-training quantization (PTQ) Nagel et al. (2020) techniques to mitigate these errors. While QAT involves retraining the model with quantization simulated during training, PTQ applies quantization directly to a pre-trained model without additional training. PTQ is particularly attractive for LLMs due to its computational efficiency, avoiding the substantial costs associated with retraining large models.

Despite advancements in PTQ methods, quantized LLMs still exhibit performance degradation compared to their full-precision counterparts, especially as quantization becomes more aggressive (e.g., 4-bit or lower). This degradation is particularly pronounced in tasks requiring complex reasoning, such as mathematical problem-solving and logical inference Dettmers et al. (2023); Xiao et al. (2023). The challenge lies in preserving the reasoning capabilities of LLMs under aggressive quantization regimes.

In this paper, we propose Quantizable Transformer (Q-Former), a novel approach designed to enhance the quantization robustness of LLMs. Q-Former introduces quantization-aware elements during pre-training, enabling the model to adapt better to quantization during inference. Specifically, our contributions are:

We introduce Quantization-Aware Low-Rank Adaptation (QA-LoRA), a technique that incorporates low-rank matrices optimized for quantization robustness during pre-training.

We propose Quantization-Guided Dynamic Activation Scaling (QDAS), a method that dynamically adjusts activation scales based on quantization sensitivity.

We develop a Quantization-Aware Training Objective (QATO) that explicitly optimizes the model for quantization robustness during pre-training.

We conduct extensive experiments on multiple LLM architectures (LLaMA Touvron et al. (2023), Mistral Jiang et al. (2023), Phi Microsoft (2023)) and quantization methods (AWQ Lin et al. (2023), GPTQ Frantar et al. (2022), SmoothQuant Xiao et al. (2023)), demonstrating consistent improvements across various quantization levels (4-bit to 8-bit) and tasks.

Our results show that Q-Former significantly reduces quantization-induced performance degradation, achieving state-of-the-art results for quantized LLMs on reasoning-intensive benchmarks. Notably, Q-Former enables 4-bit quantized models to achieve performance comparable to 8-bit baselines while reducing latency by up to 2.3× and memory footprint by up to 50%.

## 2 Related Work

### 2.1 Quantization Techniques for LLMs

Quantization techniques for LLMs can be broadly categorized into quantization-aware training (QAT) and post-training quantization (PTQ). QAT methods Hubara et al. (2017); Jacob et al. (2018) involve training the model with simulated quantization, allowing it to adapt to lower precision. However, QAT is computationally expensive and impractical for large-scale LLMs due to the substantial resources required for retraining.

PTQ methods, which quantize pre-trained models without additional training, have gained popularity for LLMs. Notable PTQ techniques include:

GPTQ Frantar et al. (2022): A layer-wise quantization method that minimizes the layer-wise reconstruction error using second-order information.

AWQ Lin et al. (2023): An activation-aware quantization approach that protects salient weights by observing activation distributions.

SmoothQuant Xiao et al. (2023): A method that migrates the quantization difficulty from activations to weights by mathematically smoothing the quantization difficulty.
